g.data-narration: Welcome to SIMD Systems - the architecture that powers parallel computing by executing the same instruction on multiple data elements.
Parallel computing solves problems faster by executing multiple computations simultaneously, forming the backbone of modern high-performance systems.
g.text: Parallel computing involves simultaneous execution of multiple computations
to solve problems faster. It's fundamental to modern computing.
Key classification: SISD | SIMD | MISD | MIMD (Flynn's Taxonomy)

g.data-narration: Flynn's Taxonomy classifies computers by instruction and data streams. SIMD stands for Single Instruction, Multiple Data - executing one operation on multiple data points.
SIMD executes one instruction on multiple data elements in lockstep. A control unit broadcasts the same operation to all processing elements simultaneously.
g.text: Core Concept: Single Instruction, Multiple Data

g.data-narration: The control processor fetches one instruction, broadcasts it to all PEs, and they execute in lockstep - all starting and finishing together.
SIMD systems have centralized control, inherent synchronization, perfect data parallelism, and rely on interconnection networks for PE communication.
Classical SIMD uses processor arrays with thousands of simple PEs. Historical examples include ILLIAC IV with 64 PEs and CM-2 with 65,536 processors.
Modern CPUs use packed SIMD with wide vector registers. A 256-bit AVX register holds eight 32-bit floats, performing eight operations per instruction.
Major CPU architectures provide SIMD extensions: x86 has MMX, SSE, and AVX; ARM has NEON. Each generation increases register width for more parallelism.
SIMD has centralized synchronous control; MIMD has distributed asynchronous control. SIMD excels at regular data-parallel problems, MIMD at irregular tasks.
g.text: GPU shader cores (in warp), AVX, NEON, ILLIAC IV

g.data-narration: SIMD offers high performance through data parallelism, energy efficiency, simpler programming model, and hardware efficiency by reusing control units.
SIMD struggles with irregular problems, requires data alignment, and faces challenges with conditional execution using predication and masking.
GPUs use SIMT (Single Instruction Multiple Threads) where warps of 32 threads execute in lockstep - the modern embodiment of SIMD principles.
